topic: Root-cause synthesis of the system-spec-kit / 026 deep-review audit

focus: Common root cause for doc/schema-to-code drift
noveltyJustification: First pass established the recurring multi-source contract drift pattern across public schemas, runtime validators, handlers, docs, and catalogs.
keyQuestions: Are schemas, handlers, docs, and catalog/playbook generated or hand-maintained from divergent sources of truth?
Is there a single generator or contract that would close most doc/schema-to-code drift?
answeredQuestions: The public tool definitions, Zod validation schemas, allowed-parameter map, handler logic, docs, feature catalog, and playbook are separate maintained surfaces.
No single generator currently closes the drift; a contract-generation or parity-test boundary would be needed.

ruledOut.approach: Treat each doc/schema mismatch as an isolated typo
ruledOut.reason: The same pattern appears across reconcile, governed ingest, causal stats, session bootstrap, catalog, and playbook surfaces.

noveltyJustification: The iteration moved from symptoms to blast radius: hundreds of 026 graph-metadata files exist and the refresh paths do not prove semantic completion truth.
keyQuestions: Is stale graph metadata and completion drift a generator/backfill defect or isolated edits?
How many packets are plausibly affected?
answeredQuestions: The root cause is mixed: canonical save updates a narrow phase-parent pointer, graph backfill refreshes discovered folders mechanically, and neither path fully verifies semantic launch/completion truth.
The blast radius is broad enough to require audit tooling: 714 graph-metadata files exist under 026 and 18 under 027 in this worktree.

ruledOut.reason: The metadata tooling refreshes many folders and has review-flag heuristics, but the observed stale last-active pointer and draft-complete state show automation does not enforce the semantic invariants operators rely on.

noveltyJustification: This pass converted review findings into operational impact: stale entity-density routing is bounded by TTL but reproducible; atomic save can create DB/file divergence on promotion failure.
keyQuestions: Do entity-density staleness and atomic-save ordering corrupt retrieval or graph-channel routing under normal single-user operation?
answeredQuestions: Entity-density staleness can misroute graph-channel activation for up to the cache TTL after update/delete because the generic mutation hook omits entity-density invalidation.
Atomic save can expose a memory-index row before file promotion, making memory retrieval point at content that is not yet durably promoted if promotion fails after indexing.

ruledOut.approach: Dismiss stale entity-density cache as harmless because Node is single-user
ruledOut.reason: The cache is process-local and TTL-bounded, but normal single-user update/delete operations still leave it stale until rebuild; the user need not be malicious.

focus: P0 security severity calibration under local MCP threat model
noveltyJustification: The security facts were already present, but severity was recalibrated against local single-user operation and governed-scope semantics.
keyQuestions: Are community fallback scope bypass and causal bare-ID tools genuine vulnerabilities or by-design under a local single-user MCP?
What calibrated severity should remediation use?
answeredQuestions: The community fallback is a genuine scoped retrieval bypass because scoped primary results are followed by global community search and ID-only member fetch before appending results.

Causal bare-ID mutation is a genuine authorization defect when tenant/user/agent scopes are security boundaries, but under a strictly local single-user MCP it calibrates better as P1 unless governed scope is explicitly promised as isolation.

ruledOut.approach: Equate P0 with remote network exploitability
ruledOut.reason: The charter's local MCP model lowers exploit likelihood, but governed scope bypass still violates explicit data-boundary contracts and can reveal or mutate out-of-scope local records.

noveltyJustification: Final pass mainly quantified blast radius and connected runtime fan-out defects to the audit artifacts themselves.
keyQuestions: Could prior deep-review/deep-research runs have silently masked failed lineages or under-delivered concurrency?
answeredQuestions: Yes. The fan-out pool counts worker returns as fulfilled even when the returned payload contains a non-zero exitCode, so orchestration summaries can report success for failed subprocesses.

The eight review slice summaries in this packet each report total_cli_lineages=1 despite five lineage report files per slice, so the merged summary files are suspect as campaign-level truth; individual lineage reports remain the better evidence surface.

ruledOut.approach: Use orchestration-summary succeeded=1 as proof all spawned lineage reports succeeded
ruledOut.reason: The fanout worker returns an exitCode object rather than throwing, so the pool summary can count the item as fulfilled regardless of subprocess status.
